text:
MEDICAL ONCOLOGY -- FOLLOW-UP VISIT NOTE
Metastatic Clear Cell Renal Cell Carcinoma

---

PATIENT: Raymond Kowalski
DOB/AGE: 63 years old
ATTENDING: [Attending Oncologist]
DEPARTMENT: Medical Oncology
VISIT TYPE: Established Patient Follow-Up

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT

Mr. Kowalski is a 63-year-old retired industrial engineer presenting today for a scheduled follow-up visit, approximately 6 weeks after initiating second-line cabozantinib for metastatic clear cell renal cell carcinoma with sarcomatoid differentiation. The primary purposes of today's visit are toxicity assessment, medication management, and clinical status update while awaiting first restaging imaging on the new regimen.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Mr. Kowalski has a known history of stage IV clear cell renal cell carcinoma (cT3bN1M1), right kidney, diagnosed approximately 9 months ago. His presentation at diagnosis included an 11.4 cm right renal mass with involvement of the right renal vein, retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy, and bilateral pulmonary metastases. He also had 22% sarcomatoid differentiation on the final nephrectomy specimen -- a feature that has been central to his clinical course. Comprehensive genomic profiling (Foundation One CDx) was notable for VHL, PBRM1, BAP1, and SETD2 loss-of-function alterations, MET amplification (copy number ~6.8), and TP53 p.R248W. His IMDC risk category is intermediate (two risk factors: time from diagnosis to systemic therapy less than one year, thrombocytosis at presentation).

He underwent cytoreductive right radical nephrectomy with retroperitoneal lymph node dissection approximately 9 months ago with an R0 resection. Postoperatively, he received first-line ipilimumab 1 mg/kg plus nivolumab 3 mg/kg every three weeks for 4 cycles, followed by nivolumab 480 mg every 4 weeks maintenance. His response to the ipi/nivo doublet was discordant: the bilateral pulmonary nodules responded nicely (the dominant right lower lobe nodule came down from 1.4 cm to a nadir around 0.7 cm), but retroperitoneal and pelvic nodal disease progressed consistently from the first restaging scan onward. After 9 months on the immunotherapy backbone, he met clear criteria for RECIST progression in the nodal compartment, and the decision was made to discontinue nivolumab and transition to cabozantinib.

The rationale for cabozantinib as second-line was multifactorial: it is standard of care in this setting, and notably his NGS identified MET amplification (copy number 6.8), which may confer enhanced sensitivity to cabozantinib given its potent MET kinase inhibitory activity in addition to VEGFR2 and AXL inhibition. He was started on cabozantinib at 60 mg orally once daily approximately 6 weeks ago.

Course on cabozantinib to date has been complicated by two significant adverse events that prompted dose modification:

1. Hand-foot syndrome (palmar-plantar erythrodysesthesia): Developed approximately 3 weeks into therapy with bilateral painful erythema, swelling, and blistering involving the palmar surfaces and plantar aspects of both feet -- consistent with CTCAE grade 2. This was limiting his ability to perform instrumental activities of daily living. Cabozantinib was dose-reduced from 60 mg to 40 mg orally daily. He was started on urea 20% cream twice daily to affected areas, instructed on cushioned insoles, and counseled to avoid tight footwear and prolonged ambulation. Concurrently, he developed grade 2 diarrhea (4-6 stools above baseline per day), managed with loperamide 4 mg initial dose then 2 mg after each loose stool, oral hydration, and low-residue diet counseling.

2. Hypertension: Approximately 5 weeks in, he was found to have blood pressure readings of 158/96 and 162/98 on two separate occasions (CTCAE grade 2). His existing antihypertensive regimen of lisinopril (which had already been uptitrated to 20 mg daily at the time of cabozantinib initiation in anticipation of VEGFR-mediated hypertension) was augmented with amlodipine 5 mg daily, subsequently uptitrated to 10 mg daily at his 2-week BP follow-up when readings remained slightly above goal.

Today, at 6 weeks on cabozantinib 40 mg daily, Mr. Kowalski reports that his toxicities have meaningfully improved since the dose reduction and initiation of supportive care measures. His first formal restaging CT has not yet been performed and is planned for approximately 3 months from cabozantinib initiation.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Constitutional: Mild fatigue, grade 1. No fevers. No night sweats. Appetite is fair -- he notes some mild taste changes that are new, though they are not interfering significantly with oral intake. Weight today 182 lbs (down from approximately 198 lbs at diagnosis; relatively stable over recent months).

Skin/Integumentary: Hand-foot syndrome now markedly improved. Reports mild residual erythema and some dryness/callous formation on bilateral palms and soles, but the blistering and pain have resolved. No new skin rashes. No nail changes.

Gastrointestinal: Diarrhea has improved substantially. Reports approximately 2-3 loose stools per day above his baseline, which he considers manageable. Using dietary modification (low-fiber diet, avoiding high-fat foods) as primary strategy; using loperamide only intermittently as needed. No abdominal cramping or pain. No nausea or vomiting. No blood in stool. No mucositis.

Cardiovascular: No chest pain or palpitations. Blood pressure control reportedly improved on current dual antihypertensive regimen. No lower extremity edema today (he had bilateral leg edema at the time of nivolumab progression, which has since resolved).

Genitourinary: No hematuria. Urinary frequency normal. No dysuria. No flank pain (this was fully resolved following cytoreductive nephrectomy).

Musculoskeletal: Mild bilateral foot soreness (residual from HFS) with prolonged standing. No bone pain.

Neurological: No headaches. No cognitive changes. No paresthesias or neuropathy.

Endocrine/Thyroid: No fatigue, cold intolerance, or other thyroid symptoms; had grade 2 hypothyroidism during ipi/nivo induction -- on stable levothyroxine dose, symptoms resolved.

Psychosocial: Overall mood acceptable. He expresses cautious optimism about starting the new medication. Engaged with his care and asking thoughtful questions about how the cabozantinib is working.

---

MEDICATIONS

1. Cabozantinib (Cabometyx) 40 mg orally once daily (dose-reduced from 60 mg due to HFS/diarrhea)
2. Levothyroxine (Synthroid) 75 mcg orally once daily (for immune-related hypothyroidism)
3. Lisinopril 20 mg orally once daily (hypertension, uptitrated at TKI initiation)
4. Amlodipine 10 mg orally once daily (added for TKI-associated hypertension)
5. Metformin 1000 mg orally twice daily (type 2 diabetes mellitus)
6. Loperamide (Imodium) 2-4 mg orally as needed for diarrhea (PRN)
7. Urea 20% cream topically to palms and soles twice daily (HFS management)

ALLERGIES: No known drug allergies.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

Vital Signs: Blood pressure 138/84 mmHg (left arm, seated); heart rate 76 bpm (regular); respiratory rate 16 breaths/min; temperature 98.4°F; oxygen saturation 97% on room air; weight 182 lbs.

General: Mr. Kowalski is a well-appearing male in no acute distress. Alert and oriented. Conversational affect, engaging appropriately. No cachexia appreciated at this visit.

Head/Eyes/Ears/Nose/Throat: Normocephalic, atraumatic. Sclerae non-icteric. Oropharynx without mucositis, oral ulcers, or thrush. No cervical lymphadenopathy.

Neck: Supple. Thyroid non-tender, not enlarged by palpation. No cervical or supraclavicular adenopathy.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No S3. Peripheral pulses 2+ bilaterally. No peripheral edema in bilateral lower extremities today (markedly improved from prior visit when he had grade 1 edema).

Respiratory: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezing, rhonchi, or crackles. Breathing non-labored.

Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. Normoactive bowel sounds. Right nephrectomy scar well-healed; no hernia or wound complications appreciated. No right upper quadrant tenderness. No hepatomegaly. No splenomegaly. No palpable abdominal masses.

Extremities / Skin: Bilateral palms and soles examined carefully. Mild residual erythema and dry, thickened skin (callous-like changes) noted on bilateral palmar surfaces and plantar aspects of both feet, consistent with resolving grade 1-2 hand-foot syndrome. No active blistering, desquamation, or open sores. Skin otherwise without new rashes. No skin nodules.

Lymph nodes: No palpable inguinal, axillary, or cervical adenopathy on exam (retroperitoneal and pelvic nodal disease not palpable).

Neurological: Grossly intact. Cranial nerves not formally tested. Gait steady. Strength and sensation grossly normal in extremities.

Performance Status: KPS 75, ECOG 1.

---

LABORATORY DATA (from today's visit)

Complete Blood Count:
- WBC: 6.1 x10^9/L (normal)
- Hemoglobin: 11.6 g/dL (mild normocytic anemia, improved from post-op nadir; stable)
- Platelets: 264,000/uL (normal; thrombocytosis resolved compared to baseline)
- Differential: no lymphopenia, no neutropenia

Comprehensive Metabolic Panel:
- Sodium: 138 mEq/L
- Potassium: 4.1 mEq/L
- Creatinine: 1.8 mg/dL (stable; baseline post-nephrectomy has been running 1.7-1.9 mg/dL)
- eGFR: approximately 42 mL/min/1.73m2 (chronic kidney disease stage IIIb, stable)
- BUN: 22 mg/dL
- Glucose: 118 mg/dL (acceptable on metformin)
- Total bilirubin: 0.6 mg/dL
- Alkaline phosphatase: 78 U/L (normal)
- ALT: 28 U/L (normal)
- AST: 31 U/L (normal)
- Albumin: 3.8 g/dL

Thyroid Function:
- TSH: 2.4 mIU/L (within normal range on current levothyroxine dose)

Lipase: 38 U/L (normal; no clinical concern for pancreatitis)

Urinalysis: No proteinuria on dipstick today. No hematuria.

Note: Fasting lipid panel deferred to next visit; patient has not been fasting today.

---

IMAGING REVIEW

No new imaging today. The most recent relevant imaging was the CT chest/abdomen/pelvis performed prior to initiating cabozantinib (at time of progression on nivolumab), which demonstrated progressive retroperitoneal and pelvic nodal disease: right common iliac nodal mass 3.1 cm, aortocaval conglomerate 3.4 cm, right obturator node 1.8 cm, right external iliac node 1.2 cm. Pulmonary lesions were relatively stable (right lower lobe 0.8 cm, left lower lobe 0.8 cm, right upper lobe 0.3 cm) and had shown favorable response during the immunotherapy phase. First restaging CT on cabozantinib is planned for approximately 3 months from treatment initiation (i.e., approximately 6 weeks from today).

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

1. METASTATIC CLEAR CELL RENAL CELL CARCINOMA WITH SARCOMATOID DIFFERENTIATION (22%), T3bN1M1, IMDC INTERMEDIATE RISK -- SECOND-LINE CABOZANTINIB, CYCLE ~6 WEEKS:

Mr. Kowalski is approximately 6 weeks into second-line cabozantinib 40 mg daily following progression of retroperitoneal and pelvic nodal disease on nivolumab maintenance (after ipilimumab/nivolumab induction). Clinically, he reports improvement in the symptoms that had accompanied his disease progression -- specifically, bilateral lower extremity edema and pelvic discomfort have resolved or markedly improved, which may reflect early therapeutic activity, though this remains subjective and must be interpreted cautiously before formal imaging response assessment. We will proceed with the planned restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast in approximately 6 weeks as scheduled.

I note that his NGS profile (MET amplification, copy number ~6.8, alongside VHL/PBRM1/BAP1 loss) makes him an interesting patient from a biological standpoint. The sarcomatoid component -- which we believe drove the nodal progression on nivolumab -- may be more VEGFR/MET-pathway driven, providing rationale for the favorable early clinical signal. We will continue to monitor.

   - Plan: Continue cabozantinib 40 mg orally once daily. No dose escalation back to 60 mg at this time given adequate clinical signal and ongoing (though manageable) toxicities. Restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with IV contrast scheduled for approximately 6 weeks from today. Routine labs at next visit.

2. HAND-FOOT SYNDROME (PALMAR-PLANTAR ERYTHRODYSESTHESIA), CTCAE GRADE 1 (IMPROVING FROM GRADE 2):

HFS has clearly improved following dose reduction from 60 mg to 40 mg and initiation of urea 20% cream twice daily. Examination today confirms resolution of blistering and active ulceration; only mild residual erythema and dry/calloused skin remain. This is an expected sequela of chronic TKI therapy.

   - Plan: Continue urea 20% cream twice daily. Continue avoiding tight footwear. Patient instructed to report any recrudescence of blistering, pain out of proportion, or secondary skin infection. Consider dermatology referral if HFS worsens or becomes challenging to manage in the outpatient setting. No further dose modification planned at this time.

3. DIARRHEA, CTCAE GRADE 1 (IMPROVING FROM GRADE 2):

Diarrhea has substantially improved from grade 2 at the time of dose reduction to current grade 1 (2-3 loose stools per day above baseline). Patient is tolerating oral intake well and there are no signs of dehydration. Dietary modifications and PRN loperamide are sufficient.

   - Plan: Continue low-residue diet modification. Continue PRN loperamide as needed (instructed on proper dosing regimen). Monitor for worsening. If grade 2 recurrence, will reassess need for further dose modification or addition of scheduled loperamide. Encourage adequate oral hydration.

4. HYPERTENSION, CTCAE GRADE 2 (IMPROVING ON DUAL ANTIHYPERTENSIVE THERAPY):

Blood pressure today is 138/84 mmHg -- improved from prior readings of 158-162/96-98 mmHg. Current regimen is lisinopril 20 mg daily plus amlodipine 10 mg daily. Goal BP for oncology patients on VEGFR-TKI therapy is generally <140/90 mmHg; he is approaching but not quite at goal. Will continue current regimen and monitor closely.

   - Plan: Continue lisinopril 20 mg daily and amlodipine 10 mg daily. Patient to perform home blood pressure monitoring at least twice weekly and report readings. If BP consistently above 140/90 mmHg, will consider addition of a third antihypertensive agent (e.g., a thiazide diuretic) in coordination with his primary care physician. Continue monitoring kidney function given lisinopril and CKD stage IIIb.

5. HYPOTHYROIDISM (IMMUNE-RELATED, FROM PRIOR IPILIMUMAB/NIVOLUMAB):

TSH 2.4 mIU/L today -- within normal limits. Patient is asymptomatic from a thyroid standpoint on levothyroxine 75 mcg daily. No symptoms of hypothyroidism or hyperthyroidism.

   - Plan: Continue levothyroxine 75 mcg daily. Recheck TSH in 3 months or sooner if new symptoms develop. No change in dose at this time.

6. TYPE 2 DIABETES MELLITUS:

Blood glucose 118 mg/dL today, acceptable. Albumin 3.8 g/dL. Patient continues metformin 1000 mg twice daily. No hypoglycemic events reported. Note: metformin is being continued cautiously given eGFR of approximately 42 mL/min/1.73m2 (marginal per FDA labeling cutoff of 30 mL/min). Renal function is stable.

   - Plan: Continue metformin at current dose. Repeat renal function at next visit. If creatinine worsens or eGFR falls below 30, will discuss dose reduction or transition to an alternative agent with his primary care provider. Encourage continued dietary management.

7. CHRONIC KIDNEY DISEASE (STAGE IIIb, POST-NEPHRECTOMY):

Creatinine 1.8 mg/dL, stable. Expected consequence of right radical nephrectomy in the setting of prior baseline creatinine 1.1 mg/dL (likely some underlying diabetic nephropathy contributing). No new proteinuria on today's urinalysis.

   - Plan: Monitor creatinine at each visit. Avoid nephrotoxic agents. Lisinopril provides nephroprotective benefit in the setting of diabetes-related CKD. Maintain adequate hydration.

8. FATIGUE, CTCAE GRADE 1:

Mild chronic fatigue consistent with ongoing systemic therapy and prior surgical and immunotherapy-related sequelae. KPS 75, ECOG 1. Performance status improved from ECOG 2 at the time of disease progression on nivolumab, which is encouraging. He is able to perform most activities of daily living without assistance.

   - Plan: Encourage regular low-impact physical activity as tolerated (walking program). Nutritional counseling referral offered. Screen for treatable contributing factors (anemia: Hgb 11.6 g/dL, stable mild anemia; thyroid function normal; depression screening negative at this visit). No pharmacologic intervention for fatigue at this time.

---

FOLLOW-UP AND COORDINATION

- Next oncology office visit: approximately 6-8 weeks (to coincide with first restaging imaging review)
- Restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with IV contrast: approximately 6 weeks from today
- Labs at next visit: CBC, CMP (with creatinine and LFTs), TSH, lipid panel
- Blood pressure home monitoring: twice weekly; patient instructed to report readings >150/95
- Patient instructed to contact oncology nurse line if: new blistering or skin breakdown, increase in diarrhea to more than 4-6 stools per day above baseline, any signs of hepatotoxicity (jaundice, RUQ pain), new severe fatigue or dyspnea, or any other concerning new symptoms
- Survivorship resources discussed; patient engaged with psychosocial support services

Time spent in direct face-to-face evaluation and counseling: approximately 40 minutes. Greater than 50% of visit time spent in counseling and coordination of care, including detailed discussion of treatment goals, toxicity management, and imaging plan.

[Electronically signed by Attending Medical Oncologist]
